Strength & Mobility — ATG Lazy Ramp

Twice a week, on Tuesdays and Thursdays. Built on the ATG (Knees Over Toes) framework by Ben Patrick. Starts at 12 minutes with three exercises and ramps to the full 25-minute Tier 01 flow over 13 weeks. The first fortnight is deliberately small — the only goal is consistency. By the end of this base block, the body is ready for ATG Tier 02 in Phase 2 of the marathon plan.

L A O · ATG COMPANION · DOSSIER 028B ISSUED 29 APR 2026

Build the body
before the mileage.

A 52-week strength and connective-tissue programme based on the ATG framework by Ben Patrick. Every exercise has a photo and most have a video tutorial — tap any "Watch" button to play the video inline. Designed to run alongside the London Marathon 2027 sub-4 plan, not compete with it.

3 → 2
Sessions / week
~25 min
Per session
15
Videos embedded
52
Weeks to race
§01

Four principles, one mindset

Read first

Why this exists

Running a marathon is a connective-tissue event before it is a cardiovascular one. The heart adapts in weeks. Tendons take months. Bones take seasons. Most amateur marathon plans put strength work as an afterthought and then wonder why knees, shins, and Achilles fail at week 30.

This plan inverts that. Strength first, mileage second. Phase 1 of the marathon is genuinely about the body's ability to absorb load. Phase 2 onwards, ATG drops to maintenance, but it never stops.

PRINCIPLE 01

Demand v Ability

Every week, every session, ask: is the load I am asking from the body matched by the ability the body has built? If demand is greater than ability, you break. If ability is greater than demand, you stagnate. The job is to keep them aligned, with ability nudging ahead.

For a returning runner post-surgery, demand starts low on purpose.

PRINCIPLE 02

Build from the ground up

Train the muscles closest to the floor first. Toes, feet, ankles, calves, then knees, then hips, then everything else. Gravity loads from the ground up, so capacity should be built the same way.

The ATG session order — sled, lower legs, step up, split squat, squat — follows this rule. Skip the lower legs and the knees pay for it.

PRINCIPLE 03

Backward rehabs forward

The effort to go forward drives the body's ability to go faster. The effort to go backward drives the body's ability to protect itself when going forward. Backward walking, backward step-ups, backward sled — these directly rehabilitate the structures stressed by running.

Twenty minutes of backward walking on rest days is non-negotiable.

PRINCIPLE 04

Strength through length

Strong muscles in their longest, deepest range protect joints better than strong muscles in their shortest range. The ATG split squat trains the quad with the back knee in deep flexion. The Romanian deadlift trains the hamstring at full stretch. The KOT calf raise trains the soleus with the knee bent.

This is what bulletproofs against the late-marathon collapse.

§02

The Flow

Order matters

Every ATG session follows the same order. The order is not optional — each step prepares the next.

Backward
5–10 min
→
02

tib + calf
→
03
Step-up
Patrick step

04
Split squat
ATG style
→
05
Squat
deep, KOT

06

hamstring

Hip + core
L-sit, couch
§03

Three Tiers · Exercises

Tap a tier
Photos & videos: each exercise card shows the movement in a photo and links to a Ben Patrick video tutorial. Tap any "Watch" button to play the video inline without leaving this page. Press Escape or click outside the video to close.

Tier 01 · Knee Ability Zero

Marathon weeks 1–14 · 3× per week · ~25 minutes per session · zero equipment

The aim is to do every exercise to the prescribed standard with bodyweight before adding any load. Most people will get there in 8–12 weeks. There is no rush.

EX 01

Backward walk

Warm-up · ground-up activation

Walk backwards. That is the entire exercise. On flat ground outside, on a treadmill at 1–3% incline, or as the warm-up to every session.

Roll through toes-to-heel, eyes ahead, posture tall. Feel it in the quads and shins.

Warm-up
5 min
Rest day
15–20 min
Frequency
Daily ok
Watch · ROKP backward walking
EX 02

Tibialis raise (wall)

Lower leg · shin protection

Stand with heels 12–18 inches from a wall. Lean back so shoulders touch the wall. Lift toes up as high as possible, lower under control. The tibialis is the most under-trained muscle in runners and the cause of most shin splints.

Control the lowering phase. Burn in the front of the shin is the point.

Sets
2
Reps
25
Tempo
slow down
Watch · tibialis raise
EX 03

Calf raise — straight knee

Lower leg · gastrocnemius

Stand on the edge of a step, heels hanging. Rise to full plantarflexion, lower until the calf is fully stretched. Both feet to start. Single-leg version once 2×25 each side feels light.

Full range — top is high, bottom is a real stretch. No bouncing.

Sets
2
Reps
25 each
Progress
→ 1 leg
Watch · calf raises
EX 04

KOT calf raise — bent knee

Lower leg · soleus

Same setup as above, but with knees bent 90°. This isolates the soleus, the deeper calf muscle and the dominant one during running. Soleus weakness is the root of most Achilles tendinopathy in runners.

Keep the bent-knee position consistent through the rep. Slow on the way down.

Sets
2
Reps
25 each
Tempo
3s down
Watch · KOT calf raise
EX 05

Patrick step (KOT step-up)

Knee · single-leg control

Stand on a step on one leg. Slowly lower the other leg toward the floor by bending the standing knee. Tap heel down lightly, return up. The knee tracks over the toes deliberately.

Foot slightly turned out. Drive through the whole foot, especially the big toe.

Sets
2 each
Reps
10 → 25
Height
low → high
EX 06

ATG split squat

Knee + hip · the keystone exercise

Front foot elevated 4–8 inches on a slant or low platform. Back leg behind in a long stance. Lower the back knee toward the floor — front knee tracks far over the toes. The deeper, the better.

Weight in the heel of the front foot at the bottom. Drive up through the front foot.

Sets
3 each
Reps
5 → 10
Goal
full depth
Why it works 60s regressions
EX 07

KOT squat (deep bodyweight squat)

Knee + hip · full range

Heels elevated on a 1–2 inch wedge or plates. Squat as deep as the body allows, knees travelling forward over the toes. Pause at the bottom for a beat. Heel elevation removes the ankle as a limiter.

Chest tall, no rounding of the lower back. The deeper, the more useful.

Sets
3
Reps
5–10
Pause
2s bottom
Watch · KOT squat form
EX 08

Elephant walk

Hamstring + posterior chain

Bend forward, plant hands on the floor (or as close as you can get). Walk slowly forward by alternating one leg straightening as the other bends. Each "step" stretches the back of the hamstring of the straightening leg under low load.

The straightening leg gets actively pushed straight — feel the back of the knee.

Sets
2
Reps
30 each
Pace
slow
EX 09

L-sit (progression)

Core + hip flexor

Sitting on the floor or seated on parallettes, hands pressing down, lift the body off the ground. Bent knees first (tuck L-sit). Then one leg straight. Then both legs straight. Hip flexors are critical late in the marathon — when they fatigue, form falls apart.

Shoulders down, chest up, push hard through the hands.

Sets
3
Hold
10s → 60s
Stage
tuck → full
EX 10

Couch stretch

Cooldown · hip flexor length

Kneel facing away from a couch or wall. Place the back foot on the surface so the shin runs vertically. Front leg in lunge. Sink hips toward floor, squeeze the back glute. Active stretch — the back glute is contracting hard.

Posterior pelvic tilt. Tuck the tailbone under. Squeeze the back glute.

Sides
2 each
Hold
60s each
When
end

Tier 01 graduation standards

Hit all six and you graduate to Tier 02 — Basics. Most people get there in 10–14 weeks of consistent work.

Tibialis raise
2×25 strict
Single-leg calf raise
25 each, full range
Patrick step
25 each leg, controlled
ATG split squat
10 reps each, full depth, no support
KOT squat
10 reps, ass-to-grass
L-sit
30s tuck or 10s straight-leg

Tier 02 · Knee Ability Basics

Marathon weeks 15–32 · 2× per week · ~30 min per session · light equipment

Same flow, same exercises, more load. The Zero work has built the tissue tolerance. Now load it. Run volume is climbing — strength sessions stay focused, not exhausting.

+ 01

Tib bar (or weighted tibialis)

Upgrade from wall tibialis raise

A tib bar (~£40 on Amazon UK) loads the tibialis directly. Sit on a bench, hook the bar over the toes, lift weight. Standard: 20% bodyweight × 20 reps for males, 16% for females.

No tib bar? Hold a dumbbell between the feet for the wall version, or use a $10 floss band.

Sets
3
Reps
15 → 20
Standard
20% BW
Watch · tib bar full breakdown
+ 02

Slant board for KOT squat

Upgrade from heel-elevated squat

A 25° slant board (~£60) lets you squat deeper with the knees over toes than any other tool. The angle removes the ankle as a limiter and forces the quads to do the work.

Light dumbbell first. Quality of range over weight on the bar.

Sets
3
Reps
8
Load
5–10kg DB
Watch · slant squat form
+ 03

Romanian deadlift (RDL)

New · hamstring strength + length

Standing with a barbell, kettlebell, or two dumbbells, hinge at the hips with a soft knee bend. Lower the load along the front of the legs until you feel a strong hamstring stretch. The further the hamstrings are stretched under load, the more protective.

Hinge, do not squat. Bar travels close to the legs. Slight knee bend, no more.

Sets
3
Reps
8
Tempo
3s eccentric
Watch · RDL pairing
+ 04

Nordic curl progression

New · eccentric hamstring

Kneel, anchor heels under a sofa or with a partner. Lower the upper body slowly toward the floor, catch with hands, push back up. Start by only lowering 30° from vertical. Progress over months. The single best exercise for hamstring injury prevention.

Tight glutes, straight line from knees to head. Slow eccentric — that is the entire point.

Sets
3
Reps
3 → 8
Range
build slowly
Beginner Full progression
+ 05

Sled drag (optional)

Optional · backward + forward

If you have access to a sled, 10 minutes alternating backward and forward drags is the single highest-value session in the whole ATG system. If not, backward walking on a treadmill at 1–3% incline is a workable substitute.

Short, choppy steps backward. Long, driving steps forward.

Time
10 min
Direction
alt fwd/back
Replaces
warm-up
Watch · sled drag

Tier 02 graduation standards

Tib bar
3×15 with 20% bodyweight
Slant squat
3×8 with 10kg DB
RDL
3×8 at bodyweight load
Nordic curl
3×5 partial range

Tier 03 · Maintenance / Pro

Marathon weeks 33–52 · 2× per week → 1× per week race week · maintenance

Marathon training is now the priority. ATG sessions get shorter, focused on the work that protects what running cannot give. Two short sessions a week, dropping to one in the final taper.

M1

The 20-minute maintenance session

2× per week · weeks 33–49

Replaces the full Zero/Basics flow. Run it Monday and Saturday, never on a quality run day or the day before a long run.

Tibialis raise 2×20 · KOT calf raise 2×20 · ATG split squat 2×6 each leg · Slant or KOT squat 2×6 · RDL 2×6 · Nordic curl 2×4 · Couch stretch 2×60s.

This is not a workout. It is insurance.

Time
20 min
Frequency
2× / week
Effort
moderate
M2

Race week — minimum effective dose

Week 52 · race week

Monday only. Tibialis raise 2×20, KOT calf raise 2×20, couch stretch, finish. No squatting, no RDL, no Nordic curl. Save the legs for race day.

Time
10 min
Day
Mon only
After
no further
M3

Backward walking — never stops

Daily-optional · all 52 weeks

The one thing that runs every week from week 1 to week 52, with no breaks, no progression changes, and no tapering. 15 minutes on rest days. 5 minutes as a warm-up before runs.

Min
3× / week
Ideal
daily
Stop
never
Watch · why backward
§04

Integration with the marathon plan

Tier × Phase
Marathon PhaseWeeksATG TierFrequencyWhy
Phase 1 — Rebuild1–12 · Apr → Jul 2026Tier 01 — Zero3× / weekTissue tolerance is the limiter. Run volume is low so strength work is the priority. Build the standards.
Phase 2 — Strength13–25 · Jul → Oct 2026Tier 02 — Basics2× / weekRun volume climbs, strength sessions get focused. Add load. RDL and Nordic curl come in.
Phase 3 — Sharpening26–39 · Oct 2026 → Jan 2027Tier 02 / 032× / weekVO2 work and MP running take priority. ATG holds the line — short, focused sessions.
Phase 4 — Specific40–51 · Feb → Apr 2027Tier 03 — Maintenance2× / weekMarathon-specific running is everything. ATG is insurance. 20-minute sessions only.
Race week52 · 19–25 Apr 2027Minimum dose1× / weekMon only, 10 minutes. Lower legs and couch stretch. Nothing else.

A typical Phase 1 week, with running and ATG laid out together. The two never compete for the same day's energy.

Mon
ATG Zero — full
~25 min
+ 15 min backward walk
Tue
Easy run
4–6 km @ 7:00/km
+ 4× strides on grass
Wed
ATG Zero — full
~25 min
No run today
Thu
Easy run
5–8 km · conversational
Fri
Rest
or 20 min backward walk
Sat
ATG Zero — full
+ easy 4 km
Sun
Long run
building 6 → 22 km
All easy. Walk breaks fine.
§06

Rules of engagement

Read once · live them
01

Pain is data, not failure

Sharp pain stops the exercise. Burn is fine, soreness is fine, sharp pain is not. The body is telling you ability is short of demand. Reduce range, reduce load, find a regression.

02

Range first, load second

Always achieve full range with bodyweight before adding any load. A weighted half-rep is worth nothing. An unweighted full-rep is worth a lot.

03

Tempo is non-negotiable

Eccentric phase (lowering) is always controlled — at least 3 seconds. The eccentric is where the connective tissue adaptation happens. Bouncing through reps misses the point.

04

Never skip lower legs

Tibialis and calves are the foundation. If you only have ten minutes, do those and skip the squats. Most runner injuries originate below the knee.

05

Backward walking is daily-optional

Five minutes as a warm-up. Fifteen minutes on rest days. It costs almost nothing in fatigue. The single highest return-on-time exercise in the entire ATG system.

06

Strength session before run, never after

If you must combine on one day, do ATG first, then easy run. Never the reverse. Tired muscles cannot produce the quality work that drives adaptation.

07

The collarbone is healed but not invisible

Avoid heavy upper-body loading until 12 months post-surgery. RDLs with light load only. No barbell overhead. Pull-ups can wait.

08

Track standards, not workouts

The progression is from Zero standards to Basics standards to Pro maintenance. Note when you hit each standard. Do not chase volume for its own sake.

§07
